feed.title: Mostafa
feed.subtitle: Thoughts, learnings, and experiences from a computer engineer chasing the fundamentals.

entry.title: Profile-Guided Optimization, From First Principles
entry.content:
This write-up was distilled from an LLVM talk about making Profile-Guided Optimization (PGO) useful for mobile apps. The talk starts from a very practical problem: mobile startup is sensitive to page faults, but traditional PGO often improves speed by increasing code size through inlining. That trade-off is awkward on mobile, where smaller code can launch faster.

The interesting idea in the talk is temporal profiling: record when functions first execute, merge many startup traces, then let the linker reorder functions so startup code sits together in the final binary. The reported results were the kind worth remembering: up to 40% fewer startup page faults, almost 1-3% compressed-size reduction, and in one Firefox iOS experiment, over 6% compressed-size reduction for the client binary from function reordering alone.

Reddit’s iOS optimization write-up is the production-scale case study that made the idea click for me. Using PGO, temporal profiling, and function reordering, Reddit reduced the main iOS binary from 198.6 MiB to 157.1 MiB and the compressed IPA from 280.6 MiB to 239.6 MiB. The numbers are impressive, but the core idea is more important: instead of asking the compiler and linker to guess what matters, you run the app, record what actually happened, and feed that evidence back into the next build.

This document builds that idea from scratch. Prerequisites: you know an app eventually becomes a binary and that a compiler turns source code into machine code. Nothing more.

Contents

  1. What the compiler normally guesses
  2. Why mobile cares about hot code and size
  3. The PGO feedback loop
  4. Coverage profiling
  5. Temporal profiling
  6. Function layout and page faults
  7. Balanced partitioning
  8. Reddit's iOS pipeline
  9. Case study: reading a real profile
  10. Measurement and traps
  11. Diagnostic reference
  12. FAQ
  13. Glossary

Part 1 — What the compiler normally guesses

A compiler has to make performance decisions before your app has ever run. Should this function be inlined? Which branch is likely? Which loop deserves more aggressive optimization? Which code should be kept close together in the final binary?

Static guess vs. profile-guided build

Static buildSource code shape
→
HeuristicsCompiler guesses hot paths
→
RiskBudget goes to the wrong code
PGO buildInstrumented app run
→
Profile dataReal hot paths recorded
→
Optimized buildBudget follows evidence

The difference is not that PGO has a smarter compiler. It gives the compiler a measured workload to believe.

Without profiles, the compiler uses static heuristics: educated guesses based on the shape of the code. Those guesses are often reasonable, but they are still guesses. A function that looks small may be irrelevant. A branch that looks unlikely may be the branch every user hits during startup. A generic utility may be cold in most of the app but hot on the first screen.

Static optimization

The compiler looks only at source/IR. It optimizes based on general rules: small functions are inline candidates, loops get transformed, branches get predicted from code shape.

Profile-guided optimization

You first build an instrumented app, run representative flows, merge the generated profile files, then rebuild using those profiles. The compiler now has evidence.

Switch the compiler's view

Source shapeSmall function, branch, loop
→
Heuristic"This probably matters"
→
RiskWrong path gets budget

Without profiles, the compiler only sees the code's shape. It cannot know which path users actually hit.

Runtime datastartup called this 1,000x
→
OptimizerSpend budget here
→
Cold pathKeep small, move later

PGO replaces "probably" with measured behavior from the workload you chose.

PGO is a feedback loop: build with instrumentation → run realistic behavior → collect profiles → rebuild using those profiles.

The profile does not change what your program means. It changes how aggressively and where the toolchain spends optimization budget.

The rule to remember: coverage PGO tells the compiler what code is hot. Temporal PGO tells the linker what code runs together. On mobile, both matter because code size and code layout affect startup.

Part 2 — Why mobile cares about hot code and size

Mobile startup is not only about how fast one function executes after it is already in memory. Before that function can run, its code has to be present in memory. If it is not, the kernel brings in the page that contains it, and that page fault costs time. A smaller and better-laid-out __TEXT section usually means fewer pages touched during startup.

This is why the hot/cold distinction matters. PGO is deciding where the toolchain should spend optimization budget. Some code deserves aggressive treatment because it runs during startup or a critical flow. Some code should mostly stay out of the way because making it bigger or placing it early can hurt the path users actually feel.

PGO starts with a simple split: some code is hot, some code is cold. Hot does not mean "important to the product" and cold does not mean "dead". Hot means "executed often, or executed during an important measured window". Cold means "rarely executed in the profile you collected".

CategoryMeaningOptimization implication
HotRuns frequently or during a critical flow such as cold startup.Worth inlining, branch tuning, layout priority, and sometimes more code size.
ColdRuns rarely or never in the collected workload.Avoid wasting binary size. Keep it out of the hot path and often optimize for size.
WarmRuns sometimes, but not in the first-order path.Optimize normally; do not let it disrupt startup layout.

The phrase "hot path" is only meaningful relative to the workload you profiled. If your profile comes from login and home-feed startup, then PGO learns about login and home-feed startup. It cannot magically optimize a checkout flow, editor flow, or obscure settings screen unless that behavior appears in the profile.

Why -Oz can beat "faster" optimization

On desktop/server code, "optimize for speed" often sounds like the obvious default. On mobile, size is part of speed. That is why mobile apps are often built with -Oz or other size-aware settings instead of blindly choosing speed-oriented optimization. More inlining can make a hot CPU loop faster, but it can also inflate code size enough to hurt cold startup.

Move the inlining dial

35%
CPU call overhead65%
Code size35%
Startup page risk42%

Inlining can reduce call overhead, but it also grows code. On mobile, too much growth can make startup worse even if individual calls are faster.

Part 3 — The PGO feedback loop

At a high level, PGO has two builds and one run in the middle.

1. Instrumented build
Compile with flags such as -fprofile-generate. The produced app includes counters or probes that write execution data.
→
2. Representative run
Exercise the app through realistic flows: startup, logged-out, logged-in, notifications, critical screens, experiments.
→
3. Merge profiles
Combine raw outputs with llvm-profdata merge into a stable .profdata file.
→
4. Optimized build
Rebuild with -fprofile-use=/path/profile.profdata. The compiler and linker now optimize with evidence.

The profiles are build inputs. Treat them like generated artifacts with a freshness policy, not like magic constants checked in forever.

Step through the feedback loop

InstrumentAdd counters/timestamps
→
ExerciseRun representative flows
→
MergeTurn raw runs into profile input
→
OptimizeCompiler/linker use the profile

The first build is deliberately instrumented; it is not the final app you ship.

clang -fprofile-generate ...       # build an instrumented binary
./run-representative-workload      # emits one or more .profraw files
llvm-profdata merge *.profraw -o app.profdata
clang -fprofile-use=app.profdata ...

Part 4 — Coverage profiling

Coverage profiling answers: what ran, and how much? It records function counts, branch behavior, and other execution-frequency signals. When you feed that profile into a later compile, the compiler can make better local decisions.

Inlining

Inlining removes a function call by copying the callee into the caller. It can expose further optimization, but it can also grow the binary. PGO helps spend inlining budget on hot calls instead of cold ones.

Branch layout

If one branch is usually taken, the compiler can lay out the common path so the CPU does less jumping and prediction is easier.

Loop and codegen choices

Loops that actually run many iterations can justify heavier optimization. Cold loops should not receive the same attention by default.

Choose a coverage profile

bootstrap()950
decodeFeed()780
openSettings()40

The compiler sees startup and feed code as hot, so inlining and branch decisions bias toward that path.

bootstrap()350
decodeFeed()120
openSettings()920

A different workload teaches a different truth. This is why representative profiling matters.

Coverage PGO is mostly about compiler decisions inside and between functions.

It tells the optimizer where the execution weight is, so it can avoid treating every path as equally important. The mobile caveat: default IRPGO is often tuned toward inlining and speed, so without size-aware settings it can grow the binary.

That caveat matters. If PGO says "this call is hot", the compiler may inline it. Sometimes that is exactly right. Sometimes the extra code size creates more instruction-cache pressure and more pages to fault in. LLVM has size-oriented knobs such as profile-guided size optimization and inlining controls for this reason. PGO is evidence; policy still matters.

Part 5 — Temporal profiling

Coverage answers "how often?". Temporal profiling answers "when, and in what order?" That distinction matters for startup. During a cold launch, the device does not load your entire binary into memory at once. It faults in pages as code is touched. If startup functions are scattered across the file, the system touches more pages earlier.

Temporal profiling records an execution trace: function A happened, then B, then C. For mobile startup, that sequence is gold. It lets the linker place functions that execute close together physically close together in the binary.

Coverage profile
FeedViewController.viewDidLoad: 1
JSONDecoder.decode: 340
SettingsScreen.open: 0
→
Compiler learns
Decode path is hot. Settings path is cold. Inline and branch decisions should reflect that.
Temporal profile
main → AppDelegate → bootstrap → feed → first cell
→
Linker learns
These functions belong near each other because startup touches them in this order.

Both profiles describe the same app, but they answer different optimization questions.

Record first-call timestamps

main
0 App
0 Feed
0 Cell
0 Settings
0

Before execution, every timestamp is zero. Zero means "not observed in this run."

In Reddit's write-up, temporal profiling used LLVM's -pgo-temporal-instrumentation. The LLVM talk explains the mechanism: every instrumented function gets a timestamp slot initialized to zero; on the first call, the runtime writes the current global timestamp and increments it. When the raw profile is dumped, functions can be sorted by timestamp to recover a trace. A zero timestamp means the function did not run in that profile.

A key detail: temporal instrumentation has much lower binary-size overhead than traditional IR PGO instrumentation, which helps the profiled app behave more like the real release app. If the profiled build is wildly different from release, its function order is less trustworthy.

Part 6 — Function layout and page faults

A binary is not just a bag of functions. It is an ordered file. That order affects cold startup because the kernel brings code into memory in pages. Reddit's article calls out 16 KiB pages on iOS. The exact page size matters less than the invariant: touching code from many far-apart regions causes more page faults than touching code from nearby regions.

Before layout
After layout

Compare page touches

Pages touched11
Startup waithigh

Startup functions are spread apart, so the launch path jumps across many pages.

Pages touched5
Startup waitlower

Startup functions are adjacent, so the launch path reuses nearby pages.

Function reordering does not make a function faster in isolation. It makes the startup walk through the binary cheaper: fewer scattered pages, fewer cold misses, less waiting before the app becomes interactive.

For cold startup, where code lives can matter almost as much as what the code does.

That is the part of PGO I want to remember: profiles can guide not only compiler transforms, but also physical binary layout.

Also keep two goals separate: the compressed IPA should be small so install/update is fast, while the installed binary should be laid out so launch is fast. The app is not decompressing the IPA during startup; compression helps distribution, page-fault reduction helps launch.

Part 7 — Balanced partitioning

The hard part is that layout has two goals that can pull in different directions.

Startup locality

Functions that run near each other in time should sit near each other in the file. This reduces page faults during cold startup and critical flows.

Compression locality

Functions with similar instruction patterns often compress better when placed near each other, because LZ-style compression benefits from repeated nearby patterns.

Pick the layout objective

Amain
Bbootstrap
Cfeed
Xsettings

Prioritize functions that execute close together in the startup trace.

X1similar bytes
X2similar bytes
Y1similar bytes
Y2similar bytes

Prioritize functions with similar instruction patterns so compression sees repeated nearby sequences.

Hot traceA · B · C
Warm similarY1 · Y2
Cold similarX1 · X2

Balanced partitioning searches for a useful compromise: startup locality first where it matters, compression locality elsewhere.

Balanced partitioning treats layout like a graph problem. Functions are nodes. Relationships between functions are utilities: "these two run near each other" or "these two look similar for compression". The algorithm recursively partitions the graph so strongly related functions tend to stay together. With an option such as --compression-sort=both, the linker can balance temporal locality and compression locality instead of choosing only one.

Temporal utility
A → B → C appeared in the startup trace, so A, B, and C should be close.
Compression utility
X and Y have similar machine-code patterns, so placing them close may shrink the compressed IPA.
Partitioning decision
Keep high-utility relationships inside the same partition when possible; split weaker relationships first.

This is why the same optimization can improve startup and reduce size. The layout is optimized using real execution traces plus code-similarity signals.

The LLVM talk gives a concrete compression model: LZ-style compression benefits from repeated instruction sequences appearing within a sliding window. Function contents can be represented by stable hashes of instructions; functions sharing many utility vertices are good candidates to place near each other. For startup, the utility vertices come from temporal traces instead.

Part 8 — Reddit's iOS pipeline

Reddit's production story becomes much easier to read once the pieces are separated.

1. Representative workloads

They reused UI performance tests around important P0 use cases: fresh install, signed-out, logged-in, account switching, opening posts, and feed paths. HTTP stubbing reduced noise so the profiles reflected app behavior instead of network timing.

2. Two profile families

Coverage profiles fed compiler optimization through -profile-use. Temporal profiles fed linker function ordering through LLD.

3. Final optimized release build

The final App Store binary was built with profile inputs, a custom LLVM linker, and balanced partitioning for startup locality and compression.

instrumented app + UI tests
  ├─ coverage .profraw files  → llvm-profdata merge → coverage.profdata
  └─ temporal .profraw files  → llvm-profdata merge → temporal.profdata

release build
  ├─ swiftc / clang use coverage.profdata for compiler PGO
  └─ LLD uses temporal.profdata for function ordering

Follow one profile through the release pipeline

Testsrealistic app paths
→
Profilescoverage + temporal
→
swiftc/clanguse coverage data
→
LLDuses temporal layout

The profile starts as UI-test behavior, not as a hand-written order file.

They also tuned the inlining threshold upward. That is an important reminder: PGO is not just one flag. It is a build pipeline. Profiles make bolder optimization choices safer, but they do not remove trade-offs. More inlining can speed hot paths and expose optimization, but it can increase binary size. In Reddit's case, the layout/compression savings created room to be more aggressive.

Results Worth Remembering

Compressed IPA
280.6 → 239.6 MiB
14.6% reduction
Uncompressed payload
359.8 → 313.1 MiB
15.3% reduction
Main binary
198.6 → 157.1 MiB
20.8% reduction

Those are size wins. The startup win was subtler to measure because iOS performs background optimizations after installation. Reddit focused on first-day behavior after install/update, where cold-start layout matters most, and used a dual-release strategy: one standard release, then an identical-code optimized release.

Part 9 — Case study: reading a real profile

Everything above was theory plus other people's numbers. So I collected an IR PGO profile from the app I work on — a large marketplace iOS app, ~100 build modules — using a UI test that performs 6 cold launches and a feed browse pass. Then I actually read the profile. This section is what the theory looks like against production-scale code, and it changed how I think about two things: how extreme the hot/cold split really is, and whose code the hot set is made of.

Functions instrumented
383,676
the whole app + every dependency
Executions recorded
36.65M
sum of every branch-point counter
Never called
96.32%
369,574 functions had entry count 0

How skewed is "skewed"?

Before looking, I assumed hot/cold meant something like 20/80. The real distribution is not in that universe. llvm-profdata show --detailed-summary prints a cumulative table: how many counter blocks (branch points) does it take to cover each share of all recorded executions? Verbatim output:

$ xcrun llvm-profdata show --detailed-summary app.profdata
Total functions: 383676
Total number of blocks: 1073586
Total count: 36654739
Detailed summary:
26 blocks (0.00%) with count >= 197216 account for 50% of the total counts.
485 blocks (0.05%) with count >= 6384 account for 90% of the total counts.
1084 blocks (0.10%) with count >= 1505 account for 95% of the total counts.
4666 blocks (0.43%) with count >= 133 account for 99% of the total counts.

Blocks needed to cover each share of execution

50% of counts26
80%181
90%485
95%1,084
99%4,666
100%24,071

Bar length = share of execution covered; the number on the right = blocks needed, out of 1,073,586. Only 24,071 blocks (2.2%) executed at all.

If execution were uniform, covering 50% of the counts would take ~537,000 blocks. It took 26. That is a concentration factor of roughly 20,000×.

This is why PGO works at all: the compiler can lavish attention on a few hundred functions and treat literally everything else as cold, and that policy is correct for 97.8% of the binary.

Whose code is hot? Not mine.

My second wrong assumption: that the hot set would be my app's code. Bucketing all 21M function entries by what kind of code was entered:

Share of function entries by code kind

Swift runtime helpers43.5%
Compiler glue37.8%
Third-party SDKs16.0%
My app's code1.5%

"Compiler glue" = protocol/value witnesses, outlined copy/destroy helpers, thunks, metadata accessors — machinery the compiler generates around everyone's code.

The single most-called function in the entire app was __swift_memcpy1_1 — 2,920,324 calls. That is 2.9 million one-byte copies, almost all driven by one third-party experimentation SDK that round-trips its JSON payloads through type-erased Any boxes during launch. The hottest single branch point in the binary was the read loop of nanopb — the C protobuf parser inside the analytics SDK — at 3.31M executions, because analytics state is parsed and re-encoded on every cold start.

Hottest spots in the binaryWhat it isMax count
buf_read (nanopb)protobuf decode loop, analytics SDK3,312,715
__swift_memcpy1_1runtime helper: copy one byte2,920,324
__swift_noop_void_returnruntime helper: do nothing2,629,953
AnyEncodable destroy/copy witnessesCodable type-erasure glue, experiments SDK535,920 / 446,600

Three more things the profile revealed that no amount of code review would have surfaced:

The profile is a free product audit

17% of all function entries belonged to one experimentation SDK's Codable glue. The localization layer burned 250K entries in just 56 functions, copying a large enum payload on every string lookup. Neither showed up in any Instruments session we had run — they were spread across too many tiny calls.

Dead weight is measurable

A generated analytics-events package contributed 6.8% of all functions in the binary but ~0.05% of execution. That is exactly the code the cold-marking half of PGO shrinks — and the strongest argument for the layout techniques in Parts 5–7.

The hot set is glue, so PGO's wins are glue wins

Inlining a 3-line value witness into its hot caller eliminates call overhead on millions of one-byte copies. The classic C wins apply too: precise branch weights in nanopb's decode loops, the hottest code in the binary.

To do this on any app: build with -fprofile-generate (plus swiftc's IR-profile flags on iOS), run a launch workload, merge, then read the result before applying it:

xcrun llvm-profdata show --detailed-summary app.profdata   # the skew table
xcrun llvm-profdata show --topn=50 app.profdata | xcrun swift-demangle   # hottest functions
xcrun llvm-profdata show --all-functions --counts app.profdata | less    # everything

Read the profile before you feed it to the compiler. The same file that guides the optimizer is the most honest launch-behavior audit you will ever get for free.

Part 10 — Measurement and traps

PGO sounds mechanical, but the quality of the result depends on the quality of the profile and the measurement plan.

TrapWhy it mattersMitigation
Unrepresentative profileThe optimizer improves the behavior you showed it, not the behavior you hoped it would infer.Profile critical user flows, feature-flag combinations, and startup states.
Profile stalenessAs code changes, old profiles mention removed functions and miss new hot paths.Regenerate profiles automatically in CI, especially before release.
Instrumentation distortionIf the instrumented app is much larger or slower, temporal order may differ from release.Prefer low-overhead temporal instrumentation for layout profiles where available.
Speed-vs-size policyInlining-heavy PGO can improve CPU-bound paths while making mobile startup worse through code growth.Use size-aware optimization settings and measure both binary size and startup/page-fault behavior.
Toolchain mismatchProfiles and compilers/linkers need compatible formats and versions.Pin toolchain versions and treat profile corruption/version errors as infrastructure failures.
Confounded rolloutIf code changes and optimization changes ship together, impact is hard to isolate.Use a controlled release plan when measuring pure optimization impact.

Tap a trap

Pick a trap to see the failure mode.

The hard engineering is not "turn on PGO". The hard engineering is collecting profiles you trust and proving the optimized binary helped.

Part 11 — Diagnostic reference

Useful commands and flags to recognize when reading a PGO pipeline:

ThingRole
-fprofile-generateBuild an instrumented binary that emits raw profile data.
LLVM_PROFILE_FILECommon runtime environment variable used to control where profile files are written.
llvm-profdata mergeMerge one or more .profraw files into a .profdata file consumed by the compiler/linker.
-fprofile-use=path / -profile-use=pathUse a merged profile during the optimized build.
-pgo-temporal-instrumentationLLVM temporal profiling instrumentation for ordering-sensitive profile data.
--compression-sort=bothLLD layout mode that balances startup-oriented ordering and compression-oriented ordering.
-OzOptimize for size. Important on mobile because code size can affect page faults and startup.
PGSO / inlining controlsSize-aware policy knobs that can mitigate binary growth from PGO-guided inlining.
pgo-warn-missing-function=falseSuppress warnings/errors when profiles mention functions not present in the final binary. Useful when profiled and release builds are close but not identical.

Command map

Build an instrumented binary that emits raw profile data. This is the learning build, not the final release build.

Part 12 — FAQ

Is PGO the same as test coverage?

No. Both may use instrumentation, but the goal differs. Test coverage asks "did tests execute this code?" PGO asks "how should the optimizer spend effort based on execution behavior?" A profile can be useful for PGO even if it is not a complete coverage report.

Does PGO require production user data?

No. Production profiles can be more representative, but they are operationally harder. Reddit chose UI tests with stable stubs and many important use cases. That is a practical trade-off: less real-world diversity, more repeatability and lower complexity.

Can PGO make code slower?

Yes. A stale or biased profile can over-optimize the wrong paths, increase code size, or damage instruction-cache locality. That is why measurement and rollback matter.

Why does binary layout affect compressed size?

Compression algorithms such as LZ variants exploit repeated patterns within a window. If similar machine-code sequences are placed near each other, the compressed artifact can shrink.

Is the app decompressing its IPA during startup?

No. The compressed IPA matters for download and installation. Startup page faults come from touching installed binary pages that are not resident in memory yet. These are related optimization goals, but not the same mechanism.

Why not always use speed optimization with PGO?

Because mobile startup is sensitive to code size. A speed-oriented profile can encourage more inlining, which can increase the amount of code the app has to page in. For mobile, the best policy often balances speed, size, and layout.

Why is startup impact strongest after install or update?

Because the first launches touch cold filesystem and memory state. Over time, OS-level caching and background optimizations can reduce the visible difference, so first-day metrics are often the cleanest window.

Quick check

If the optimized app has a smaller compressed IPA but the same startup page faults, which half of the optimization probably helped?

Compression locality. Startup locality is measured through page faults and time-to-interactive behavior; compressed IPA size is a distribution/install win.

Part 13 — Glossary

Pick a term

A feedback loop: collect execution behavior from an instrumented run, then use it to optimize a later build.
PGO
Profile-Guided Optimization: optimizing a later build using profiles collected from an earlier instrumented run.
IRPGO
Instrumentation-based PGO at the LLVM IR level. The compiler inserts instrumentation into intermediate representation to collect execution data.
Coverage profile
A profile describing which functions, branches, and paths ran and how often.
Temporal profile
A profile describing execution order over time, useful for function layout.
Function reordering
Changing the order of functions in the final binary so related code is physically close.
Page fault
An event where the system must bring a memory page into the process because the app touched code or data not currently resident.
LLD
LLVM's linker. In this context, it consumes temporal profiles and performs function layout optimizations.
Profile staleness
The loss of profile usefulness as source code and behavior drift away from the workload that produced the profile.

Sources

  • Reddit Engineering: Reddit's iOS App Binary Optimization, written by Karim Alweheshy.
  • YouTube / LLVM talk: temporal profiling and balanced partitioning for mobile PGO.
  • LLVM Clang Users Manual: Profile Guided Optimization.
  • LLVM discourse RFC: Temporal profiling extension for IRPGO.

entry.title: iOS Code Signing, From First Principles
entry.content:
This write-up was distilled from a real session: wiring a CI pipeline to ship a ShowCase app (a design-system demo app) to TestFlight, debugged end-to-end with an AI agent. The release build failed with no provisioning profile was found named 'match AppStore com.example.designkit.ShowCase'. A profile was downloaded to “fix” it — and turned out to be the wrong type. Untangling that one error required understanding the entire chain: certificates, App IDs, devices, profiles, the four distribution channels, fastlane match, and where a profile has to physically sit for a build to find it. This document builds every concept from scratch and ends with a reference you can reuse. Prerequisites: you’ve built an iOS app once and know what an .ipa roughly is. Nothing more.

Contents

  1. The cast of five
  2. Certificates: who you are
  3. App IDs & entitlements: what the app is
  4. The provisioning profile: the knot
  5. Four profile types, two flags
  6. Channels: how a build reaches a human
  7. fastlane match: signing for teams
  8. Where the profile must physically be
  9. Case study: the ShowCase failure
  10. Diagnostic reference
  11. FAQ
  12. Glossary

Part 1 — The cast of five

Apple code signing answers one question at install/launch time: "Is this exact app allowed to run on this device, and who vouches for it?" Everything else is bookkeeping that supports that check. There are exactly five primitives. Learn what each one binds and the rest follows.

1. Team / Account

Your Apple Developer Program membership, identified by a Team ID (e.g. ABCDE12345). Everything below is scoped to one team. Two apps in the same team share certificates; two apps in different accounts share nothing.

2. Certificate

Proves who built the app. A public/private key pair where Apple signs your public key. Team-scoped, not app-scoped.

3. App ID

The app's identity — its bundle id (com.example.designkit.ShowCase) plus the capabilities it's allowed to use.

4. Device

A specific iPhone/iPad registered by UDID. Only matters for two of the four profile types. Capped at 100 per device class per year.

5. Provisioning profile

The document that ties the other four together and is signed by Apple. This is the piece everyone finds confusing, because it's the knot — so we build up to it.

The mental model: a certificate says who, an App ID says what, devices say where, and a provisioning profile is Apple's signed statement that this who + what + where combination is permitted.

Part 2 — Certificates: who you are

A signing certificate is a public/private key pair. You generate the pair locally; the private key never leaves your Keychain. You send a Certificate Signing Request (the public half) to Apple, and Apple hands back a certificate — essentially your public key with Apple's signature on it, saying "this key belongs to this team."

When you sign an app, codesign uses the private key to produce a signature over the app bundle. When iOS verifies the app, it checks that signature against the certificate embedded (by reference) in the provisioning profile. No private key → you cannot sign. No matching certificate in the profile → the device rejects the signature.

Two kinds matter:

CertificatePurposeScope
Apple DevelopmentSign debug builds you run from Xcode on your own registered devices.Per-developer (each dev usually has their own).
Apple DistributionSign everything you ship: Ad-Hoc, TestFlight, App Store.One per team, shared. The same distribution cert signs every app in the team.

Certificates are team-scoped, never app-scoped.

This is why, in our session, the existing Apple Distribution certificate could sign a brand-new app (com.example.designkit.ShowCase) without anything new — as long as the app lived in the same team. A new bundle id does not require a new certificate. (It does require a new profile — see Part 4.)

Practical gotcha: Apple limits the number of distribution certificates per account (historically ~2–3). If every engineer and every CI machine generated their own, you'd exhaust the limit and break each other's signing. That problem is exactly what fastlane match exists to solve.

Part 3 — App IDs & entitlements: what the app is

An App ID registers a bundle id with Apple and records which capabilities it may use (Push Notifications, Associated Domains, Keychain Sharing, App Groups, …). Two forms:

  • Explicit: com.example.designkit.ShowCase — matches exactly one app. Required for capabilities like Push.
  • Wildcard: com.example.* — matches a family of apps, but can't carry most capabilities.

Entitlements are the runtime permissions your app actually requests (in an .entitlements file). The rule the OS enforces: the entitlements baked into your signed app must be a subset of what the App ID enables and what the provisioning profile grants. Ask for an entitlement the profile doesn't carry → install fails. This is a common source of "it ran in debug but the release build won't install."

Part 4 — The provisioning profile: the knot

A provisioning profile is a single file (.mobileprovision) that is a CMS-signed property list. "Signed" means Apple has cryptographically endorsed its contents — you can't edit it. Inside, it bundles:

embedded.mobileprovision — what's inside
One or more certificates (the allowed signers) · exactly one App ID (bundle id + entitlements) · a device list (for some types) · the Team ID · an expiry date · a UUID and a human Name.
↓ at build time
codesign signs the .app with your private key, and copies the profile into the bundle as embedded.mobileprovision.
↓ at install / launch
iOS checks: signature matches a cert in the profile? bundle id matches the App ID? device in the list (or channel allows all)? entitlements ⊆ profile? profile not expired? All must pass.

The profile is the contract the device evaluates. It is the only one of the five primitives that travels inside the shipped app.

Two consequences that trip everyone up:

  • Profiles are bundle-id-scoped. A profile for com.example.OtherApp can never sign com.example.designkit.ShowCase, even in the same team with the same certificate. A new app always needs a new profile.
  • Profiles expire (typically one year). The cert can be valid while the profile is stale, or vice-versa. "It built last quarter" is not evidence it builds today.

Part 5 — Four profile types, two flags

There are four profile types, and you can tell them apart by just two fields inside the decoded plist: get-task-allow (is the app debuggable?) and ProvisionedDevices (is there a UDID allowlist?).

Typeget-task-allowProvisionedDevicesSigned withUsed for
Developmenttrueyes (your devices)Apple DevelopmentXcode run/debug
Ad-Hocfalseyes (allowlist)Apple DistributionOTA install to listed devices
App StorefalsenoneApple DistributionTestFlight & App Store
Enterprisefalsenone, but ProvisionsAllDevices=trueIn-House DistributionInternal distribution (Apple Enterprise Program only)

The single most useful distinction in practice:

Ad-Hoc and App Store profiles are both signed with the same distribution certificate and both have get-task-allow=false. They differ in one thing: Ad-Hoc has a device allowlist, App Store does not.

That one difference decides everything downstream. A device list means "only these phones." No device list means "this goes through Apple's store infrastructure (TestFlight/App Store), which controls distribution instead." You cannot mix them: an Ad-Hoc profile cannot be uploaded to TestFlight, and an App Store profile cannot sideload onto a random device.

Part 6 — Channels: how a build reaches a human

"How do I get the app onto someone's phone?" has four answers, and each is locked to a profile type. Choosing the channel is choosing the profile.

Development

Xcode → your own registered device, with the debugger attached. Devices must be in the profile. For engineers, not testers.

Ad-Hoc

Build an .ipa, host it on an OTA install page (Bitrise public install page, Firebase App Distribution, Diawi…). Every tester's UDID must be registered and the profile regenerated. Hard cap ~100 devices/year/class.

TestFlight

Upload an App-Store-signed build to App Store Connect. Testers join by email / group — no UDID. Up to 100 internal + 10,000 external testers (external needs a light review).

App Store

Same App Store profile, but submitted for full App Review and released publicly.

If the destination is TestFlight, the profile must be App Store. Full stop.

This is the crux of the whole session. The goal was "designers install the ShowCase app via TestFlight." TestFlight only accepts App-Store-signed builds, so Ad-Hoc was never an option — even though Ad-Hoc feels simpler. The payoff for App Store signing is huge for this use case: zero UDID management. Designers come and go; nobody wants to register and re-register phones.

Part 7 — fastlane match: signing for teams

Everything above works for one developer on one Mac. It falls apart for a team + CI, for two reasons: (1) Apple limits distribution certificates, so you can't let everyone generate their own; (2) certificates depend on a private key that lives in one Keychain — a CI machine that didn't generate the key can't sign at all.

fastlane match solves this with one idea: store the shared distribution certificate (including its private key) and all the provisioning profiles in a private, encrypted git repository. Every machine — laptops and CI — runs match to download and install the same identity instead of creating new ones.

Private repo ios-match
encrypted .p12 (cert+key) + .mobileprovision files
→ fastlane match on any machine →
Keychain gets the cert+key
profiles land in ~/Library/MobileDevice/Provisioning Profiles/

One source of truth, encrypted with a passphrase. New CI box? Run match, you're signing in seconds.

match also imposes a naming convention on the profiles it creates: match {Type} {bundleid} — e.g. match AppStore com.example.designkit.ShowCase. That exact string becomes the profile's Name, which matters in the next part. A subtle trap we hit: the app_identifier list in the Matchfile is only a default; if your Fastfile lanes call match(app_identifier: [...]) with an explicit list, that list wins and the Matchfile is ignored. Adding a new bundle id means editing the lane, not just the Matchfile.

Part 8 — Where the profile must physically be

Creating a profile in the portal (or in the match repo) is not enough. The signing tool has to find it on the machine doing the build. There is one canonical location:

~/Library/MobileDevice/Provisioning Profiles/*.mobileprovision

How it gets there, and how a build refers to it, depends on the toolchain:

ToolchainHow it finds the profile
Xcode (automatic)Manages and installs profiles for you, matching by bundle id + capabilities.
fastlane matchDownloads from the encrypted repo into the folder above.
Bitrise CIThe certificate-and-profile-installer step installs whatever profiles/certs are uploaded to the Bitrise app's "Code Signing & Files" store into that folder.
Bazel (rules_apple local_provisioning_profile)Searches the folder above by the profile's Name field — not by bundle id, not by file path.

Bazel resolves the profile by name, so the name in your BUILD config must equal the Name field inside the actual installed profile.

Our build config asked for match AppStore com.example.designkit.ShowCase (the match convention). A hand-made portal profile named showcase-adhoc-testflight would not be found even if it were the right type — the names don't match. Either name the portal profile to match, or point the build config at the real name.

Part 9 — Case study: the ShowCase failure

Now the whole session reads cleanly. The pipeline ran a release (App Store) build of //ShowCase:ShowCaseApp and died:

error: no provisioning profile was found named 'match AppStore com.example.designkit.ShowCase'

Correct and expected: the certificate (team-scoped) was reusable, but the profile (bundle-id-scoped) for this brand-new app didn't exist anywhere yet — not in the portal, not in the match repo, not installed on the runner.

A profile was then downloaded to unblock it. The right move before trusting any .mobileprovision is to decode and inspect it:

security cms -D -i showcase-testflight.mobileprovision > /tmp/p.plist
plutil -extract Name        raw -o - /tmp/p.plist   # showcase-adhoc-testflight
plutil -extract TeamIdentifier.0 raw -o - /tmp/p.plist # ABCDE12345  ✅
plutil -extract Entitlements.get-task-allow raw -o - /tmp/p.plist # false
plutil -extract ProvisionedDevices raw -o - /tmp/p.plist # 69  ← devices!

Team ✅, bundle id ✅ — but get-task-allow=false and a 69-device ProvisionedDevices list. Cross-reference the table in Part 5: that is an Ad-Hoc profile, despite the word "testflight" in its filename. App Store Connect would reject it. The fix wasn't to install it — it was to go back to the portal and create the App Store distribution type (Distribution → App Store Connect, no device selection) for the same App ID.

A profile's filename is a human label and lies freely. Its type lives in two fields you must decode to see.

Part 10 — Diagnostic reference

Keep these. They turn "signing is magic" into "signing is a plist I can read."

Decode any profile to readable XML:

security cms -D -i Some.mobileprovision        # prints the plist

Identify a profile's type (run after piping the decode to a file, e.g. /tmp/p.plist):

plutil -extract Name              raw -o - /tmp/p.plist
plutil -extract AppIDName         raw -o - /tmp/p.plist
plutil -extract TeamIdentifier.0  raw -o - /tmp/p.plist
plutil -extract Entitlements.application-identifier raw -o - /tmp/p.plist
plutil -extract Entitlements.get-task-allow         raw -o - /tmp/p.plist
plutil -extract ExpirationDate    raw -o - /tmp/p.plist
plutil -extract ProvisionedDevices raw -o - /tmp/p.plist  # errors/absent ⇒ App Store; a number ⇒ Ad-Hoc/Dev
plutil -extract ProvisionsAllDevices raw -o - /tmp/p.plist # true ⇒ Enterprise

List what's installed on this machine:

ls -1 ~/Library/MobileDevice/Provisioning\ Profiles/
# then decode each to read its Name

Inspect signing certificates in your Keychain:

security find-identity -v -p codesigning   # lists usable signing identities

See how an already-built app was signed:

codesign -dvvv --entitlements :- /path/to/Your.app

FAQ

I added a new app to the same team. Do I need a new certificate?

No. Certificates are team-scoped. The existing Apple Distribution certificate signs any app in the team. You do need a new App ID and a new provisioning profile, because both are bundle-id-scoped.

Why can't I just use my Ad-Hoc profile for TestFlight? It's distribution-signed.

TestFlight distribution is handled by Apple's store infrastructure, which requires an App Store profile (no device list). An Ad-Hoc profile carries a UDID allowlist and is meant for direct OTA installs; App Store Connect rejects it on upload.

The build says it can't find a profile, but I can see it in the Apple portal.

Existing in the portal ≠ installed on the build machine. It must be downloaded into ~/Library/MobileDevice/Provisioning Profiles/ (via Xcode, fastlane match, or a CI installer step). And if you build with Bazel, the profile's Name must match what your BUILD config asks for.

Development vs Ad-Hoc — both have my devices. What's the difference?

get-task-allow. Development profiles set it true (the app is debuggable and signed with a development cert); Ad-Hoc sets it false (release behaviour, signed with the distribution cert). Same device list, different runtime posture and certificate.

How long do these last?

Provisioning profiles typically expire after one year; certificates similarly. They expire independently, so always check ExpirationDate rather than assuming "it worked before."

Glossary

Team ID
Identifier for your Apple Developer account (e.g. ABCDE12345). The scope for certificates.
Certificate
A public/private key pair endorsed by Apple, proving who signed an app. Development (per-dev) or Distribution (team-shared).
Private key
The secret half of a certificate, kept in the Keychain. Without it you cannot sign — which is why CI needs it shared (see match).
App ID
A registered bundle id plus its allowed capabilities. Explicit or wildcard.
Bundle id
The app's unique reverse-DNS identifier, e.g. com.example.designkit.ShowCase.
Entitlements
Runtime permissions the app requests; must be a subset of what the App ID and profile grant.
UDID
Unique identifier of a physical device. Listed in Development and Ad-Hoc profiles.
Provisioning profile
Apple-signed file binding certificate(s) + App ID + (maybe) devices + entitlements + team. Embedded in the shipped app as embedded.mobileprovision.
get-task-allow
Entitlement flag; true = debuggable (Development), false = release (Ad-Hoc / App Store).
ProvisionedDevices
The UDID allowlist inside a profile. Present ⇒ Development or Ad-Hoc. Absent ⇒ App Store.
Ad-Hoc
Distribution profile with a device allowlist; for direct OTA installs, not TestFlight.
App Store profile
Distribution profile with no device list; required for TestFlight and the App Store.
TestFlight
Apple's beta distribution service. Accepts App-Store-signed builds; testers join by email, no UDID.
fastlane match
Stores the shared certificate and profiles in an encrypted git repo so all machines install the same signing identity. Names profiles match {Type} {bundleid}.
codesign
The macOS tool that signs an app bundle with your private key and embeds the profile.
local_provisioning_profile
The rules_apple Bazel rule that finds an installed profile by its Name field.

entry.title: Mean, Median, and Percentiles, From First Principles
entry.content:
This write-up was distilled from a real session reading a build dashboard: three numbers — mean 11.2 min, P50 8.35 min, P90 19.8 min — and one question, do these even make sense? The document below builds every concept needed to answer it from the ground up: what a mean and a median actually measure, why latency is always uneven, what percentiles add, and how to sanity-check a set of summary numbers in seconds. Prerequisites: you can add and divide. Nothing more is assumed.

Contents

  1. Two ways to summarize a list
  2. Why latency is uneven
  3. Percentiles: P50, P90, P99
  4. The shape, drawn
  5. Sanity-checking a set of numbers
  6. Which number to use when
  7. Things people get wrong
  8. Glossary

Part 1 — Two ways to summarize a list of numbers

You have many measurements — build durations, request latencies, page loads — and you want one number that says "this is what's typical". There are two common choices, and they answer subtly different questions.

Mean (the average)

The arithmetic mean of n values is

x̄ = 1n Σi=1n xi

Every point enters the sum, which is its weakness: a single large xi shifts x̄ by Δxi / n. The mean is the distribution's center of mass.

Median (the middle, a.k.a. P50)

Sort the values x(1) ≤ x(2) ≤ … ≤ x(n); the median is the middle order statistic:

x̃ ={x((n+1)/2),  n odd½(x(n/2) + x(n/2+1)),  n even

It depends only on position, not magnitude — so a single huge value barely moves it.

The example that shows the difference

Five builds finish in x = (6, 7, 8, 9, 40) minutes (one was a cold-cache straggler):

x̄ = 6+7+8+9+405 = 705 = 14,    x̃ = x(3) = 8

Four of five builds finished under 10 minutes, yet x̄ = 14 — the lone 40 dragged it up, while x̃ = 8 stayed put. The mean is sensitive to outliers; the median is robust to them.

x̄ > x̃  ⟺  long tail on the high side (right skew)

The gap measures the pull. Our build numbers give x̄ − x̃ = 11.2 − 8.35 = 2.85 min, a ratio of x̄ / x̃ = 11.2 / 8.35 ≈ 1.34 — a slow minority pulling the mean ~34% above the median. The normal signature of timing data (Part 2).

Part 2 — Why latency is (almost) always uneven

Build times, request times, any "how long did it take" metric share a structural property: they are right-skewed — a tall cluster of fast results with a long tail trailing off to the right.

There is a floor, but no ceiling

A build can't take less than zero time, and in practice not less than some minimum (compile the world, link, etc.). But it can take arbitrarily long: a cold cache, a busy machine, a flaky network download, a retry. The fast side is bounded; the slow side is open-ended. That asymmetry is the skew.

Consequence: x̄ ≥ x̃, by default

The high-side tail pulls the center-of-mass while the middle position holds, so for healthy latency you should expect

x̄ ≥ x̃

It is not a defect to fix — it is the shape of the phenomenon.

A hard floor on the left, an open-ended tail on the right: that asymmetry is the right-skew, and it is what pushes the mean to the right of the median.

Reframe: the surprising case is x̄ < x̃. For a duration metric that almost never happens naturally — if you see it, suspect the metric is computed wrong, units are mixed, or negative/clamped values leaked in.

Part 3 — Percentiles: P50, P90, P99

A percentile answers one plain question: compared to everything else, how far up the list is this? Sort your builds from fastest to slowest — P90 is the point that 90% of builds come in under. Only the slowest 10% take longer.

Make it concrete. Here are five build times, in the order they finished: 8, 5, 19, 6, 9 minutes. Line them up fastest → slowest:

5   6   8   9   19
  • The middle value is 8 — that is the median, also called P50. Half the builds are faster, half are slower. The "typical" build.
  • The average is (5+6+8+9+19) ÷ 5 = 9.4 — bigger than the median, because the one slow 19 drags it up. That gap between 9.4 and 8 is exactly the problem with averages from Part 1.
  • P90 is the same move, but at 90% instead of 50%: walk up the sorted list until you have passed 90% of the builds. With lots of data it is "the line that 9 out of 10 builds beat" — it lives out in the slow tail.

Now imagine doing this for hundreds of builds. Walk left → right through every build time, and each time you pass one build, step up a little. The height at any point is just "what fraction of builds have I passed so far?" — that rising staircase is the picture below. To read any percentile, find its height on the left, slide across to the steps, and drop down to the time.

Each build nudges the line up one step. To find P90: start at 90% on the left, slide across to the staircase, then drop to the time below it. P50 is the median; P90 and P99 sit further out in the slow tail.

The same idea, written as a formula

Let F(x) be the fraction of samples at or below x — exactly the height of the staircase above. Then the k-th percentile is the smallest x whose height has reached k/100:

Pk = inf { x : F(x) ≥ k100 },    x̃ = P50

"inf" just means "the smallest value that qualifies" — it only matters for the flat parts of the staircase, where many x share the same height.

P50 — the typical case

Half of builds are faster than this. The everyday experience. Same thing as the median.

P90 — the "bad day"

9 of every 10 builds beat this; the slowest 10% are worse. This is the number that captures "sometimes it just takes forever" without being thrown off by the single worst outlier.

P99 — the worst-case-ish

Only 1 in 100 is slower. Matters when rare slowness is expensive (CI queues, user-facing requests at scale). Not in our three numbers, but worth requesting.

Key property: percentiles are positions in the sorted data, so — like the median — they are robust to outliers. A single catastrophic build moves P99 a little and P50 not at all. That robustness is exactly why SLOs are written on percentiles, not on the mean.

Part 4 — The shape, drawn

Here is the distribution of build durations. Toggle between a hypothetical symmetric dataset and the skewed one our real numbers describe, and watch the three markers move relative to each other.

Right-skewed: a tall cluster of fast builds and a long tail. The mean (yellow) is dragged right of the median (blue) by that tail; P90 (red) sits far out in it.

P50 · Median
8.35 min
The typical build. Half finish faster.
Mean · Average
11.2 min
Pulled up by the slow tail.
P90 · Tail
19.8 min
9 of 10 beat this; slowest 10% are worse.

Part 5 — Sanity-checking a set of numbers

Back to the original question: do mean 11.2, P50 8.35, P90 19.8 make sense? Two quick checks settle it.

Ordering check passes

For right-skewed latency the only sensible order is P50 < x̄ < P90. Ours:

8.35 < 11.2 < 19.8  ✓

x̄ above the median is the expected fingerprint of skew (Part 2). Had x̄ < 8.35, that would be the red flag worth chasing.

Tail check worth a look

Two dimensionless ratios gauge how uneven the data is:

P90P50 = 19.88.35 ≈ 2.37spread    x̄P50 = 11.28.35 ≈ 1.34skew

The slowest 10% take well over double a typical build. Both ratios sit in the normal range (most real systems land 2–4×), but the tail is where time is actually lost.

Verdict: the numbers are internally consistent and realistic. They describe an ordinary, healthy distribution — most builds near 8 minutes, an average nudged to ~11 by a slow minority, a "bad day" near 20.

The one thing to act on is that 8 → 20 minute tail, not the headline average.

Part 6 — Which number to use when

NumberUse it forWhy
P50Headline "how fast are builds" / daily developer experienceReflects the common case, immune to rare outliers.
P90 / P95SLOs, alerting thresholds, "worst-case" budgetingCatches tail regressions before they become "builds randomly hang" complaints.
P99 / maxRare-but-expensive slowness at scaleThe true worst case lives past P90; request it when the tail is costly.
MeanAlmost nothing, on its ownOne 60-min build moves it without telling you whether the typical case or the tail changed. Useful only paired with the median as a skew signal.
General rule: report a percentile for decisions, keep the mean only as a companion to the median. A dashboard showing the mean alone hides whether your typical build or your tail got worse.

Part 7 — Things people get wrong

"I'll just average the percentiles to combine two services."

First, what "combine" means here: you have build times measured separately for two things — say a Linux runner pool and a macOS pool, or the app and core targets in a monorepo — and you want one P90 across both. The tempting shortcut is to average the two P90s your dashboard already shows. It doesn't work, because a percentile is a position in the sorted data, not a quantity you can add or average.

Counterexample: pool A is ten builds all at 2 min (so P90 = 2), pool B is ten builds at 10 min (P90 = 10). Averaging the two gives 6 min — but dump all 20 builds into one pile and the real combined P90 is 10 min. Build volume tips it further: if core builds 2,000×/day and app builds 10×/day, the combined P90 is essentially core's — one pool dominates the pile, so averaging the two numbers as equals is meaningless.

P90(A ∪ B) ≠ ½(P90(A) + P90(B))

You also can't recover the mean x̄ from P50 and P90: those say nothing about the top 10%, where the mean's weight sits. Two pools with the same P50 and P90 can have wildly different averages — one's slowest builds might top out at 25 min, another's drag out to hours.

Each percentile is its own independent cut of the data. To combine pools or projects, go back to the raw build durations (pool them and recompute), or keep a mergeable summary per pool like a histogram or t-digest and merge those before reading the percentile — never do arithmetic on the finished P90 numbers.

"Mean and median are basically the same thing."

Only for symmetric data (toggle the chart in Part 4 to see them coincide). For anything with a tail — which is all latency — they diverge, and the gap is information: it tells you how skewed the data is.

"A 2.37× P90/P50 ratio looks bad."

It's typical, not bad. Build and request systems commonly sit between 2× and 4×. A ratio drifting toward 5×+ signals an unstable tail worth investigating; a ratio near 1× would mean unusually consistent timing. 2.37× is normal variability with clear room to tighten.

"These three numbers are enough."

Three things are missing: the sample count and time window (11.2 min over 50 builds is far less trustworthy than over 50,000), the P99 / max (the real worst case lives past P90), and a trend over time (a single snapshot can't say whether builds are getting faster or slower).

Glossary

mean (average)
x̄ = (1/n) Σi xi. Uses every point; sensitive to outliers. The distribution's center of mass.
median (P50)
x̃ = P50, the middle order statistic of the sorted data — half below, half above. Robust to outliers.
percentile (Pk)
Pk = inf{ x : F(x) ≥ k/100 }, the value below which k% of measurements fall. P50 is the median.
right-skew
A distribution with a long high-side tail and a low-side bound. Produces x̄ > x̃. The natural shape of latency data.
tail
The slow minority of measurements (e.g. the slowest 10%, captured by P90). Where most "it's sometimes slow" pain lives.
SLO
Service Level Objective — a target almost always written on a percentile (e.g. P95 < 15 min) because percentiles are robust to single outliers.
spread / skew ratio
P90/P50 measures tail spread; x̄/P50 measures skew. Quick gauges of how uneven a distribution is.

Summary

The mean is a balance point and moves with every outlier; the median and other percentiles are positions and ignore magnitude. Latency data has a floor but no ceiling, so it is right-skewed, so its mean sits above its median by default. To sanity-check a set of summary numbers, confirm the ordering (P50 < Mean < P90 for skewed data) and read the gaps as skew. Make decisions on percentiles; keep the mean only as a companion that reveals skew.

Build numbers verified consistent: P50 8.35 · Mean 11.2 · P90 19.8 min · June 2026

entry.title: Clang Modules and Bazel, From First Principles
entry.content:
This write-up was distilled from a real migration session: enabling explicit Clang modules (swift.use_c_modules) in a large iOS monorepo, debugged end-to-end with an AI agent. One build flag flipped; nine builds failed; none of the failing code had changed. The document below builds every concept needed to understand why — header resolution, module maps, PCMs, Bazel’s action model, aspects — and then walks through each failure as a case study. Prerequisites: you can read Swift/Objective-C and know Bazel has “rules”. Nothing more is assumed.

Contents

  1. Header imports and search paths
  2. Module maps and PCMs
  3. Bazel: the minimum working model
  4. Implicit vs. explicit compilation
  5. The governing invariant
  6. Case studies: nine failures
  7. Root-cause classification
  8. Diagnostic reference
  9. FAQ
  10. Glossary

Part 1 — How a header import is resolved

Everything in this document reduces to one question: given an #import line, where does the compiler look for the file? There are two import forms and three search mechanisms.

Textual inclusion

#import "X.h" and #import <Y/Y.h> are preprocessor directives. By default the preprocessor replaces the line with the full text of the file, recursively. A translation unit (one .m file) expands to itself plus every header it transitively includes — frequently 100k+ lines — re-parsed for every translation unit.

Quote include: #import "X.h"

Resolution order:

  • The directory of the including file ("includer-relative"). "../Shared/X.h" resolves here without any flags.
  • Then each directory passed with -I, in order.

A quote include naming a file in a different directory only works if some -I flag points there. This is the root cause of case 6.

Angle include: #import <Y/Z.h>

Resolution order:

  • Each -I directory: looks for <dir>/Y/Z.h.
  • Each -F directory (framework search path): looks for <dir>/Y.framework/Headers/Z.h.
  • System/SDK paths.

#import <FBSDKCoreKit_Basics/FBSDKCoreKit_Basics.h> requires a -F flag pointing at the directory containing FBSDKCoreKit_Basics.framework. Root cause of case 1.

Framework layout

A built framework is a directory with fixed structure:

FBSDKCoreKit.framework/
├── FBSDKCoreKit          (binary)
├── Headers/              (public headers)
│   └── FBSDKCoreKit.h
└── Modules/
    └── module.modulemap  (Part 2)

An xcframework is a wrapper directory holding one such framework per platform slice (device, simulator, …).

Resolution is just this walk down a list of locations. The green steps need no target-private flags; every other step depends on a -I or -F that must be present in the running compilation — which is exactly what changes when a module is compiled in isolation (Parts 4–5).

The consequence that matters

Header resolution is governed entirely by which flags are on the command line of the compilation currently running. The same header can resolve in one compilation and fail in another with no change to any file — only the flag set differs. The entire migration is about which compilation parses each header, and therefore which flags are in effect.

Part 2 — Module maps and PCMs

Textual inclusion has two structural problems: cost (every consumer re-parses every header) and fragility (a header's meaning depends on whatever macros and flags the consumer happens to have). Clang modules address both.

The module map

A module.modulemap file declares that a set of headers forms a named unit:

framework module FBSDKCoreKit {
  umbrella header "FBSDKCoreKit.h"   // this header (and its includes) define the module's contents
  export *                            // importers also see what this module's headers import
  module * { export * }              // one submodule per header under the umbrella
}
  • umbrella header — a header that includes all other public headers; the module's contents are whatever it declares.
  • framework module — header paths resolve inside the framework's Headers/ directory.
  • Non-framework module maps list headers explicitly: module LookinServer { header "A.h" header "B.h" }.

The PCM (precompiled module)

To "build a module," the compiler parses the headers named by the module map — in a compilation of its own, with its own flag set — and serializes the resulting AST to a binary .pcm file. Importers deserialize the PCM instead of re-parsing headers. Analogous to .c → .o: parse once, reuse many times.

How imports change

@import FBSDKCoreKit; (ObjC) and import FBSDKCoreKit (Swift) load the module by name. With modules enabled, even a textual-looking #import <FBSDKCoreKit/FBSDKCoreKit.h> is remapped to a module import when a module map claims that header. Swift has no headers at all — every ObjC dependency reaches Swift through a module, which is why module correctness is non-negotiable in a mixed codebase.

Module-level dependencies

Building module A's PCM may itself encounter #import <B/B.h>. If B is covered by a module map, A's PCM records a dependency on module B, and B's PCM must be available. Modules therefore form a dependency graph of their own, parallel to the build system's target graph. The migration's central problem: these two graphs must agree, and under the old mode nothing checked that they did.

The two open questions

The module map says nothing about when the PCM is built or which flags that build sees. Two strategies exist:

  • Implicit — the compiler builds PCMs on demand, mid-compilation (Part 4, left panel).
  • Explicit — the build system builds every PCM as a scheduled step (Part 4, right panel).

The migration is the switch from the first to the second.

The umbrella header creates a module-graph edge, but RSPM generated the binary target with deps = [], so the matching target-graph edge is missing. Implicit mode never compared the two; explicit mode compiles each module in isolation and the gap becomes case 1's file not found.

Part 3 — Bazel: the minimum working model

Six concepts are sufficient to read everything that follows.

1 · Targets and labels

A target is a named buildable unit declared in a BUILD file, addressed by a label: //Modules/Networking:Networking means "target Networking in package Modules/Networking". Labels starting with @ address targets in external repositories — downloaded dependencies, e.g. @swiftpkg_facebook_ios_sdk//:FacebookCore.

2 · Rules and attributes

Each target is an instance of a rule (swift_library, objc_library, apple_dynamic_xcframework_import) with attributes:

  • srcs, hdrs — source and header files.
  • deps — labels of targets this target depends on. This is the build graph.
  • copts — extra compiler flags, private to this target's own compilations.
  • includes — header search dirs that propagate: dependents get them automatically.
  • features — named toolchain behaviors; "x" enables, "-x" disables, per target or globally.

3 · Actions and sandboxing

Building a target produces actions: concrete command lines (one compiler invocation each) with declared inputs and outputs. Actions run sandboxed — the command can only read files declared as inputs. An undeclared file is absent, not merely discouraged. Actions are cacheable (locally and remotely) and reproducible precisely because their inputs are closed.

4 · Providers

Targets pass structured data to dependents through providers. Two matter here:

  • CcInfo — C-family compilation context: headers, defines, -I/-F search paths. A target's copts are not in CcInfo; includes are.
  • SwiftInfo — Swift modules and, under explicit compilation, the Clang modules (module map + PCM) a target exposes.

"X propagates to dependents" always means "X travels in a provider along deps edges."

5 · Aspects

An aspect walks the dependency graph and attaches additional actions and providers to existing targets without editing their BUILD files. The relevant one is rules_swift's swift_clang_module_aspect: it visits every target reachable through deps, and for each target that owns a module map, registers a SwiftPrecompileCModule action that builds the PCM. Error messages name it explicitly: "from aspects [swift_clang_module_aspect] on objc_library rule target …".

6 · Generated external repos (RSPM)

Swift Package Manager dependencies are integrated by rules_swift_package_manager (RSPM): it downloads each package, reads its manifest, and generates a BUILD file — one target per SPM target. A binary SPM target (binaryTarget, a prebuilt xcframework) becomes an apple_*_xcframework_import target. Critical limitation: SPM's manifest format gives binary targets no dependency list, so RSPM generates them with deps = []. RSPM ≥ 1.17 adds configure_package(target_deps = {...}) to inject edges into the generated BUILD by hand.

Part 4 — Implicit vs. explicit compilation

Same sources, same module maps. The difference is who schedules PCM compilation and which flags it sees. The migration enables two features in .bazelrc: swift.emit_c_module and swift.use_c_modules.

Step 1 — Bazel schedules ONE action: the consumer's Swift compile all search paths merged
swiftc compiles RudderManager.swift. Its target's deps are many, so the merged CcInfo yields:
-F facebook  -F braze  -F zendesk  -F sentry  -F firebase  -I lookin/Shared  … one command line, everything visible
↓ during that compile, swiftc encounters import FBSDKCoreKit and no PCM exists yet
Step 2 — the compiler builds the PCM itself, mid-compilation
It finds the module map via the -F paths, then parses FBSDKCoreKit.h. That parse hits #import <FBSDKCoreKit_Basics/…>
resolves — this parse runs INSIDE the consumer's compilation and inherits its full flag set (Part 1: resolution = current flags).
The PCM lands in a compiler-private cache, outside Bazel: ~/…/ModuleCache/<hash>/FBSDKCoreKit-<hash>.pcm

Properties of implicit compilation. (1) PCM builds are not Bazel actions: invisible to the action graph, excluded from remote caching, re-done per machine and per flag-hash. (2) The flag set used to build a PCM is whatever the first importer had — module content can vary by import order and configuration. (3) Header resolution inside a module build borrows the consumer's search paths, so a module's undeclared dependency on a sibling resolves successfully whenever any consumer's flags cover it. No mechanism verifies that the module graph matches the target graph.

Step 0 — swift_clang_module_aspect walks the deps graph and schedules one PCM action per module map
Each SwiftPrecompileCModule action's inputs = the owning target's files + its declared deps' CcInfo/SwiftInfo. Nothing else exists inside the sandbox.
PCM: FBSDKCoreKit_Basics OK
Needs no other module → compiles from its own framework files alone.
PCM: FBSDKCoreKit FAILS
deps = [] (SPM binary target, Part 3 §6)
→ no -F path to the sibling in this sandbox
#import <FBSDKCoreKit_Basics/…> → file not found
PCM: BrazeKit independent
Own action, own sandbox, runs in parallel.
↓ Swift compiles are scheduled only after the PCMs they need exist
Consumer compile receives finished artifacts — it never parses dependency headers
-fmodule-map-file=…/module.modulemap  -fmodule-file=FBSDKCoreKit=bazel-out/…/FBSDKCoreKit.pcm
Each PCM is an ordinary Bazel action: sandboxed, deterministic per configuration, remote-cacheable, shared across machines.

Properties of explicit compilation. (1) Every module map reachable through deps is precompiled — whether or not any source imports it (relevant in case 2). (2) Each PCM build sees only declared inputs; an undeclared dependency is a hard error rather than a silent borrow. (3) PCM actions are cached and shared org-wide, and run in parallel ahead of Swift compiles. The cost: every assumption the implicit mode silently absorbed must now hold explicitly. The nine case studies are exactly those assumptions.

Part 5 — The governing invariant

Under explicit compilation, each module map is compiled in an isolated, sandboxed action whose visible inputs are the owning target's files plus its declared dependencies' providers. Consumer search paths do not exist inside that sandbox.

Corollary (root cause of cases 6 and 8): when the aspect constructs a PCM action for a target, it takes the compilation context from CcInfo — and forwards only -D defines from the target's copts. Since copts never enter CcInfo (Part 3 §4), any -I search path supplied via copts is absent from the PCM compile. Search paths reach a PCM action only through the includes attribute or a dependency's CcInfo.

Same import, two worlds. The consumer compile sees every dependency's search paths merged together, so a sibling resolves incidentally; the isolated PCM action sees only declared inputs, so the identical line fails. That difference is the entire migration.

Part 6 — Case studies: nine failures, in build order

Each entry names the failing action, explains why the same code compiled under implicit mode, derives the root cause from Parts 1–5, and documents the fix.

01Facebook SDK — 'FBSDKCoreKit_Basics.h' file not foundUndeclared edges between sibling binary frameworks · fixed via RSPM target_deps

Failing action

PCM precompile of FBSDKCoreKit. Its umbrella header contains an angle include of a sibling framework, #import <FBSDKCoreKit_Basics/FBSDKCoreKit_Basics.h>, which requires a -F path to the directory containing FBSDKCoreKit_Basics.framework (Part 1).

Why it compiled under implicit mode

The PCM was built inside a consumer's compilation, which had every Facebook framework's -F path merged from its many deps (Part 4, implicit panel, step 2).

Root cause

SPM binary targets carry no dependency list, so RSPM generated deps = [] (Part 3 §6). The isolated PCM action's sandbox contains only FBSDKCoreKit's own files — the sibling framework is not an input, so its headers do not exist there (Part 3 §3).

Fix

Upgraded RSPM 1.13.0 → 1.19.0 (the target_deps API landed in 1.17.0) and declared the SDK's internal layering in MODULE.bazel:

swift_deps.configure_package(
    name = "facebook-ios-sdk",
    target_deps = {
        "FBSDKCoreKit":           ["FBAEMKit", "FBSDKCoreKit_Basics"],
        "FBAEMKit":               ["FBSDKCoreKit_Basics"],
        "FBSDKLoginKit":          ["FBSDKCoreKit"],
        "FBSDKShareKit":          ["FBSDKCoreKit"],
        "FBSDKGamingServicesKit": ["FBSDKCoreKit", "FBSDKShareKit"],
    },
)

With the edges declared, the sibling's framework directory becomes a sandbox input and its CcInfo supplies the -F path; the dependency PCM is also built first, in topological order.

General rule: the module graph (who imports whom at the header level) must be mirrored by the target graph (deps). Implicit mode never checked this; explicit mode enforces it.
02GoogleAppMeasurement — umbrella header not foundModule map present, headers absent · fixed via rules_apple patch

Failing action

PCM precompile of GoogleAppMeasurement. The framework ships a module map whose umbrella header directive names GoogleAppMeasurement-umbrella.h — but the framework contains no Headers/ directory at all. The referenced file does not exist in the vendor's artifact.

Why it compiled under implicit mode

It never compiled — and never needed to. No source imports this module; the framework is link-only. Implicit PCM builds are triggered by imports (Part 4), so an unimported module map is never parsed. The defect was inert.

Root cause

Explicit-mode property (1): the aspect precompiles every module map reachable in the graph, imported or not. Invalid metadata that implicit mode never evaluated now fails a scheduled action.

Fix

A patch to rules_apple's xcframework import rules: when the imported framework ships no headers, do not create the Swift interop provider, so the aspect schedules no PCM action for it. Correct because a headerless framework has no importable surface; linking information is unaffected.

General rule: eager precompilation validates all module metadata in the dependency graph, including metadata for modules that no source imports.
03Zendesk / LiveRamp / Braze — module 'SDKConfigurations' not foundCross-package edges; imports hidden in -Swift.h and .swiftinterface · fixed via target_deps with public labels

Failing actions

Two kinds. (a) PCM precompile of MessagingSDK: binary frameworks containing Swift code ship a generated ObjC header (MessagingSDK-Swift.h) that re-exposes their Swift API to ObjC; it is part of the umbrella, and it contains @import SDKConfigurations; — a module import of a module from a different SPM package. (b) Compilation of MessagingAPI's .swiftinterface: frameworks with Swift API ship a textual interface file that rules_apple compiles into a binary swiftmodule, and that compilation must resolve every import statement in the file.

Root cause

Identical to case 1 — binary targets, no declared edges — extended across package boundaries. The required modules live in other generated external repositories (Part 3 §6).

Implementation notes

① A binary framework's true dependency set spans three file types: ObjC headers, the generated -Swift.h, and .swiftinterface files. The initial header-only audit missed the latter two; the complete audit found MessagingSDK → SDKConfigurations + CommonUISDK + MessagingAPI, SupportSDK → five Zendesk modules, BrazeLocation → BrazeKit, LRAtsSDKMediationAdapter → LRAtsSDK + GoogleMobileAds. ② Generated .rspm targets have package-private visibility; cross-package target_deps values must use each repo's public product labels, e.g. @swiftpkg_core_sdk_ios//:ZendeskCoreSDK — referencing the .rspm targets directly fails Bazel's visibility check.

General rule: audit ObjC headers, -Swift.h, and .swiftinterface imports to determine a binary framework's dependency set. Any one alone is incomplete.
04Firebase umbrella — 'FirebaseCore/FirebaseCore.h' file not foundThe required edge would create a cycle · fixed via __has_include source patch

Failing action

PCM precompile of the Firebase umbrella module — a source target whose only purpose is letting app code write import Firebase. Its header unconditionally angle-includes FirebaseCore's umbrella.

Why target_deps does not apply

The build graph already contains the reverse edge: FirebaseCore depends on Firebase (it consumes the Firebase_VERSION define from it). Bazel's target graph must be acyclic — adding Firebase → FirebaseCore is rejected at analysis time. This is the one failure where "declare the edge" is structurally impossible.

Fix

A source patch (RSPM configure_package(patches) applies patches to package sources after download, before BUILD generation) making the include conditional:

-#import <FirebaseCore/FirebaseCore.h>
+#if __has_include(<FirebaseCore/FirebaseCore.h>)
+  #import <FirebaseCore/FirebaseCore.h>
+#endif

__has_include evaluates against the current compilation's search paths (Part 1): true in every consumer compile (unchanged behavior), false in the isolated PCM action (import skipped, module compiles near-empty). Acceptable because no source in the repository imports Firebase; the rest of that header already uses the same guard pattern.

General rule: when a required module edge would create a target-graph cycle, make the header conditional with __has_include instead of modifying the graph.
05Sentry — type argument 'SentrySpan' must be a pointerA never-compiled fallback branch is invalid ObjC · fixed via target_deps on the clang child target

Failing action

PCM precompile of SentryInternal, a source target in sentry-cocoa. The header:

#if __has_include(<Sentry/Sentry.h>)
#  import <Sentry/Sentry.h>        // declares: @protocol SentrySpan
#endif
…
@class SentrySpan;                    // fallback: forward-declares a CLASS of the same name
@interface SentryTracer : NSObject <SentrySpan>

ObjC keeps class and protocol names in separate namespaces. When the protocol exists, <SentrySpan> in that position resolves to it — valid. When only the forward-declared class exists, Clang parses NSObject<SentrySpan> as a generic specialization with a non-pointer type argument — the reported diagnostics. The fallback branch is invalid code that no compilation had ever selected.

Root cause

The isolated PCM action has no path to <Sentry/Sentry.h> (no declared edge to the Sentry framework), so __has_include evaluates false for the first time in this codebase's history, selecting the broken branch.

Fix

Declare the edge so the primary branch is selected. One subtlety: RSPM splits a mixed target into a parent plus per-language child targets; the failing action belongs to the generated clang child SentryInternal.rspm_objc. target_deps keys containing .rspm are matched against generated target names verbatim, allowing the edge to be attached exactly where the action runs: "SentryInternal.rspm_objc": ["Sentry"].

General rule: __has_include fallback branches are unexercised code paths. The isolated PCM compile is often the first compilation ever to execute them; expect latent invalidity.
06LookinServer — 'LookinDefines.h' file not foundHeaders depend on copts -I, which never reaches the PCM action · fixed via include-path rewrite

Failing action
